nativeRange.habitat: Shallow sandy-bottomed protected areas, reef flats, and rocky substrates in tropical and subtropical coastal waters
nativeRange.biotope: Marine benthic; anchors via rhizoids (holdfasts) into sandbed or rockwork; found in the photic zone in sheltered coastal environments

imageCandidates.notes: Robin White; 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ shot of upright fronds showing the diagnostic cactus-like pinnate branchlets; whole plants framed over a sandy seagrass flat.
Thomas Irvine; 946x1024. Freshly collected green sprig on a white tray, tack-sharp and showing the branching cactus form and short branchlets in clear detail (out of water).
Jared Shorma; 2048x1536. Multiple green clumps in shallow water showing dense cactus-like branching growth habit; lightly sand-dusted.

summary: Cactus Caulerpa is a slow-growing ornamental saltwater macroalgae with distinctive upright fronds that branch in a cactus-like pattern. Well suited to display aquariums, it requires careful management: like all Caulerpa species, it produces chemicals that can stress corals and is capable of a sudden reproductive crash.
careNotes:
Cactus Caulerpa (Caulerpa cupressoides) is a siphonous green macroalgae — meaning its entire body is a single, highly branched cell without internal walls — named for its striking resemblance to terrestrial cactus. Long stolons (horizontal runners that anchor into sand or rock) give rise to thick upright stalks branching into dense rows of short branchlets. Size has been reported to range from 2 to 25 cm depending on growth conditions and variety; several botanical varieties exist, including var. cupressoides, elegans, flabellata, lycopodium, and mamillosa.

ALLELOPATHY: Like all Caulerpa species, C. cupressoides produces secondary metabolites — including caulerpenyne and related sesquiterpenes — as chemical defenses against herbivory. These compounds can stress corals and competing algae when released into the water column. Physical separation from sensitive SPS corals (the small-polyp stony corals most reactive to chemical irritants) and use of activated carbon are generally advisable precautions.

GOING SEXUAL / REPRODUCTIVE CRASH: All Caulerpa species can undergo holocarpy — a mass reproductive event in which the organism converts its entire cytoplasm into gametes and effectively dies. This can release large quantities of organic matter into the water column, causing an acute water-quality crash capable of harming or killing other tank inhabitants. Triggers include sharp nutrient drops, disrupted photoperiod, or rapid temperature swings. To reduce the risk: run refugium lighting on a 24-hour cycle to suppress reproductive triggering; harvest regularly to keep the colony in active vegetative growth; maintain stable (but not near-zero) nutrient levels; and remove any specimen showing early signs of going sexual (fronds turning white or translucent, or a milky discharge into the water) immediately.

Captive specimens must never be released into natural waterways. Caulerpa cupressoides is considered an invasive concern outside its native range.

High lighting is generally considered preferable. Moderate water flow is generally advised. Stable reef water parameters — NSW-level salinity and consistent temperature — are recommended.

GROWTH RATE NOTE: This species tends to grow slowly relative to other Caulerpas such as C. prolifera or C. racemosa, making it better suited as an ornamental display specimen than as a primary nutrient-export workhorse.

See also [caulerpa-prolifera] for the Atlantic and Mediterranean blade-form relative that shares the same allelopathy and crash caveats, and [sea-grapes] for the grape-cluster display Caulerpa.
breedingNotes: Propagates in the aquarium via fragmentation — cuttings of the stolon or upright fronds have been reported to establish as new plants — and by vegetative tip growth along the stolon.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby trade source for this species. Provided growth form description, placement (display), growth rate (slow), invasive status, shipping/color-recovery notes, and general compatibility.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Caulerpa cupressoides
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmed: Family Caulerpaceae, Order Bryopsidales, Class Ulvophyceae. Native range (global tropical/subtropical: Americas, Africa, Indo-Pacific, Australia) confirmed. Morphology: coenocytic, stolon-based with cactus-like branching uprights, 2–25 cm. Sesquiterpene allelopathy documented as herbivore deterrent. Varieties: var. cupressoides, elegans, flabellata, lycopodium, mamillosa.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
